patch_headlines.title: San Ramon Art & Wind Festival: What To Know
San Ramon Art & Wind Festival Returns This Weekend
San Ramon Hosting Memorial Day Celebration At Memorial Park
San Ramon, SRVUSD Students Named National Merit Scholars
Third Thursday Arts & Eats Debuts In San Ramon
San Ramon Council To View FY26-27 Preliminary Budget

stories.headline: Adarsh Orders Royal Audit After San Ramon Council Previews FY26-27 Budget
stories.dek: The King of San Ramon says every kingdom needs fiscal discipline, especially one funded mostly by cafeteria coins.
stories.patch_headline: San Ramon Council To View FY26-27 Preliminary Budget
body: After Patch reported that San Ramon officials would review the city's preliminary FY26-27 budget, Adarsh Upadhyaya announced that the Kingdom of San Ramon would conduct its own parallel audit from a notebook labeled 'Important Royal Stuff.'

The 9th grader's finance team, consisting of two classmates and a calculator app, identified several priorities: defense against Dublin, emergency boba reserves, and a line item titled 'mysterious but necessary snacks.' Adarsh reportedly asked whether Measure N could be adapted into Measure A, for Adarsh, before being told that is not how municipal finance works.

The King of Dublin immediately accused San Ramon of overspending on ceremonial pens, escalating tensions along the I-580 corridor. Adarsh's office responded that all pens were 'essential infrastructure' and promised a balanced budget by the end of lunch.

stories.headline: King Congratulates San Ramon National Merit Scholars, Declares Brainpower Strategic Asset
stories.dek: Adarsh praises local academic excellence while quietly asking if scholarships can be paid in royal influence.
stories.patch_headline: San Ramon, SRVUSD Students Named National Merit Scholars
body: Following Patch's report that San Ramon and SRVUSD students were named National Merit Scholars, Adarsh Upadhyaya issued a formal statement applauding the winners and inviting them to advise the royal court on finals-week survival.

Sources say Adarsh was especially interested in their study methods, asking whether a color-coded planner could defeat the King of Dublin in open academic combat. The scholars declined to enter the I-580 War but did recommend sleeping before tests, a suggestion the court described as 'radical but intriguing.'

By afternoon, the Kingdom of San Ramon had created a new advisory council called the Merit Table. Its first agenda item was congratulating the winners; its second was figuring out how to make Adarsh's backpack less loud during math.

stories.headline: Third Thursday Arts & Eats Becomes Royal Cultural Program After Adarsh Endorsement
stories.dek: Food trucks, local art, and live music are now officially part of the San Ramon soft-power strategy.
stories.patch_headline: Third Thursday Arts & Eats Debuts In San Ramon
body: After Patch covered the debut of Third Thursday Arts & Eats in San Ramon, Adarsh Upadhyaya declared the summer series a 'cultural victory' for the kingdom and asked whether his court could reserve a table near the most strategic taco truck.

The event's mix of food trucks, local art, live music, and hands-on activities reportedly appealed to the King's entire governing philosophy: snacks first, diplomacy second, vibes always. Advisors believe the series could help San Ramon project cultural strength without directly provoking Dublin.

The King of Dublin attempted to respond by announcing a rival 'Fourth Friday Chips & Arguments,' but attendance projections remain unclear. Adarsh's camp says it will monitor the situation closely while supporting local artists and possibly ordering churros.

stories.headline: Adarsh Calls For Respectful Silence At San Ramon Memorial Day Ceremony
stories.dek: The royal court pauses jokes to honor the real purpose of Memorial Day at Memorial Park.
stories.patch_headline: San Ramon Hosting Memorial Day Celebration At Memorial Park
body: After Patch reported that San Ramon would host a Memorial Day celebration at Memorial Park, Adarsh Upadhyaya reminded the Kingdom of San Ramon that not every public appearance is about royal drama.

The ceremony, featuring patriotic music, tributes, and a flag-placing ceremony at the Commemorative Field of Flags, was described by the King's office as 'a moment for respect, not lore.' Even the usually combative Dublin faction agreed to observe a temporary ceasefire for the day.

Adarsh reportedly told classmates that honoring service members matters more than any rivalry with Dublin. The statement was widely praised by the court, though one advisor admitted they still checked whether Arjun Mehta had subtweeted anything afterward.

stories.headline: King of San Ramon to Attend Art & Wind Festival Under Heavy Security
stories.dek: Royal entourage confirms kite diplomacy amid Dublin spy rumors.
stories.patch_headline: San Ramon Art & Wind Festival Returns This Weekend
body: Following coverage in Patch San Ramon of this weekend's Art & Wind Festival, Adarsh Upadhyaya's court announced the King will make a public appearance — his first at a municipal event since the I-580 ceasefire talks stalled in Danville.

Sources say Adarsh plans to evaluate the professionally choreographed kite demonstrations for 'strategic aerial potential,' while avoiding any direct line of sight with Dublin scouts rumored to be posing as funnel-cake vendors. The King of Dublin, Arjun Mehta, has not commented, though his group chat status remains aggressively online.

Festival organizers told reporters they welcome all attendees, royal or otherwise, provided no one tries to crown anyone near the face-painting booth. Security will be handled by volunteers, one parent, and Coach Martinez, who said he 'has dodgeballs if needed.'

stories.headline: King of San Ramon Brokers Historic Truce Between Lunch Lines B and C
stories.dek: Sources say a single raised eyebrow ended a conflict that had simmered since September.
body: Quarry Lane High School's cafeteria reached a turning point Thursday when Adarsh Upadhyaya, the undisputed King of San Ramon, stepped between rival lunch lines and restored order with nothing more than a calm nod and the words, "We share the tater tots."

Witnesses report that Line B had been encroaching on Line C's mozzarella-stick territory for weeks, creating tension among 9th graders who previously considered themselves neutral. "He didn't even raise his voice," said one student who asked to remain anonymous because their allegiance is "complicated." Administrators declined to comment, citing an ongoing investigation into whether tater tots qualify as a diplomatic gift.

By period five, both lines had resumed normal spacing, and a handmade sign reading "SAN RAMON UNITED" appeared above the condiment station. The King's office — understood to be the table near the windows — issued no formal statement, though classmates confirmed he finished his lunch and homework in the same sitting, a feat the student council called "aspirational."

stories.headline: Royal Decree Issued in San Ramon Group Chat: "No Spoilers Before 9 PM"
stories.dek: The King's first policy of the semester targets a threat more dangerous than any pop quiz.
body: In a move that legal scholars at Quarry Lane High School are still debating, Adarsh Upadhyaya posted a binding decree to the Kingdom of San Ramon group chat late Tuesday: spoilers for popular shows must not appear before 9:00 PM local time, under penalty of being muted until further notice.

The policy emerged after a contentious incident involving a single emoji reaction that some residents interpreted as a major plot reveal. "We needed structure," explained one neighbor who lives on the same block and attends the same algebra class. "He's not asking for much. Just basic civilization."

Compliance has been strong, though a minor rebellion in a subgroup was quickly dissolved when the King replied with a GIF that sources describe as "devastatingly effective." Analysts say the decree may set precedent for future regulations on memes, homework reminders, and who gets to pick the playlist on bus rides.

stories.headline: PE Class Alliance Formed After King of San Ramon Voluntarily Runs Extra Lap
stories.dek: Motivation experts baffled by 9th grader who sprints without being chased.
body: During sixth-period physical education, Adarsh Upadhyaya surprised classmates and faculty alike by completing an optional extra lap around the track, an act so unprecedented that three students reportedly stopped mid-stretch to watch.

Coach Martinez later told reporters that the King's effort had a "cascade effect," prompting others to jog rather than walk and reducing complaints about the weather by an estimated 40 percent. "I don't know what he ate for breakfast," Martinez said, "but I'd like to order it in bulk."

The newly formed "San Ramon Fitness Coalition" — not an official team name, but trending anyway — has already requested matching wristbands. The King has neither confirmed nor denied plans to captain a relay team, though sources close to his backpack say he owns at least two pairs of running shoes.

stories.headline: Science Fair Project on "Optimal Homework Snack Velocity" Wins Quiet Applause
stories.dek: Adarsh Upadhyaya's research asks: how fast must one eat a granola bar to finish math?
body: Quarry Lane's annual science fair featured volcanoes, robots, and one entry that stopped judges in their tracks: Adarsh Upadhyaya's study measuring the relationship between snack consumption speed and algebra completion rates.

Using a stopwatch, a control group of willing friends, and what he described as "rigorous chip-bag methodology," the King of San Ramon presented data suggesting that moderate-paced snacking yields the best quiz scores. "I hypothesized that panic-eating would hurt performance," he told the panel, "and the numbers agree."

The project did not win first place — that went to a solar-powered phone charger — but it earned a special mention for "Most Relatable Research." Classmates have already requested peer-reviewed follow-ups involving iced coffee and late-night study sessions.

stories.headline: Bus Stop Summit Resolves Long-Standing Seat Claim Dispute
stories.dek: The streets of San Ramon see peace at last.
body: A months-long dispute over who gets the "good seat" at the morning bus stop — the one with minimal sun glare and optimal phone signal — was settled Friday when Adarsh Upadhyaya convened an impromptu summit before first bell.

Attendees say the King proposed a rotating schedule tied to whoever arrived earliest, with exceptions for rain and "legitimately heavy backpacks." The agreement was ratified by handshake and one commemorative photo that may or may not become a mural.

Residents of the San Ramon kingdom report improved morning morale and a 15 percent decrease in eye-rolling. Transportation officials had no comment, as they were not invited and are still confused about why they weren't.

stories.headline: Vending Machine Treaty Signed After King Mediates Chip vs. Candy Conflict
stories.dek: Economic stability returns to the B-wing hallway.
body: Tensions flared near the B-wing vending machines when supply of spicy chips ran low just as demand for chocolate peaked. Into the breach stepped Adarsh Upadhyaya, who suggested a simple rule: no hoarding, and always leave one item for the person behind you.

The treaty, scribbled on the back of a permission slip, was signed by seven students and one teacher who happened to be passing by. "It's not the Geneva Convention," the teacher said, "but it's closer than you'd think for a Tuesday."
Since the agreement, restocking days have become orderly affairs, and the King has been spotted explaining coin-return etiquette to confused freshmen. Economists remain unavailable for comment.

stories.headline: Debate Club Invites King of San Ramon to Argue Side "Pro: More Recess"
stories.dek: Organizers hope the King can settle what Parliament — and the bell schedule — cannot.
body: Quarry Lane's debate club extended a formal invitation to Adarsh Upadhyaya after his impromptu hallway argument in favor of extended recess went viral among 9th graders, racking up eleven verbal "likes" and zero official endorsements.

Club president Riley Nguyen said the King brings "a gravitas we usually only see during election season." The proposed resolution: whether five extra minutes of outdoor time would improve academic focus or merely delay the inevitable.

Adarsh has not publicly accepted, though insiders say he is drafting talking points on a napkin. Faculty advisors welcome the discourse but remind all parties that the bell schedule remains, as one administrator put it, "not up for royal veto."
